candidate_problems.text: Is the claimed monotonic growth of bureaucratic complexity actually true, or are there identifiable conditions under which complexity can genuinely decline for long periods rather than merely reappear in shifted form? This is the key empirical and conceptual vulnerability of the conjecture: it needs a measurable notion of 'complexity,' a way to distinguish relocation from reduction, and an account of possible counterexamples such as state capacity reforms, codification, digitization, or regime resets.

What exact game structure generates complexity as an equilibrium, and which assumptions are doing the real work: repeated interaction, asymmetric blame, judicial review, adversarial exploitation, principal-agent problems, or low-trust turnover? The conjecture bundles many mechanisms together; separating them is crucial for turning it from an evocative narrative into a testable theory and for identifying the true leverage points for reform.

Can governments create durable substitutes for procedural complexity—such as reputation systems, safe harbors for good-faith discretion, better observability, or institutional trust mechanisms—or does scale, heterogeneity, and political contestation make formalization irreducible beyond small high-trust domains? This is the main design question implied by the conjecture: whether complexity is merely adaptive to current incentives or partly a necessary coordination technology in large modern states.
